examples.title: Lamport Logical Clock Implementation
examples.description: Algorithm for maintaining Lamport logical clocks across processes to capture happened-before relationship
examples.explanation: Lamport clocks provide a total ordering consistent with causality. Each event increments the counter. When sending, counter increments. When receiving, take max of local and received timestamp then increment. If A→B (causally), then L(A) < L(B), but L(A) < L(B) doesn't imply causality (could be concurrent).

examples.title: Two-Phase Commit Protocol - Coordinator
examples.description: Coordinator side implementation of 2PC for atomic distributed transaction commitment
examples.explanation: The coordinator first sends PREPARE to all participants. If all vote YES, it sends COMMIT; otherwise sends ABORT. The blocking problem occurs if coordinator fails after participants have voted YES - they wait indefinitely for the final decision.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n = number of participants

examples.title: Vector Clock Implementation
examples.description: Implementation of vector clocks to track causal history across multiple processes
examples.explanation: Vector clocks capture full causal history. If all components of A's vector ≤ B's vector, and at least one is strictly less, then A causally precedes B. If neither precedes the other, they are concurrent. Used in Dynamo, Cassandra for conflict resolution.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) per message receive, O(n) for causality check
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) per process for vector storage
